question.content: A vertical square column has a fixed base at A and a free top at C, with total height l and cross-section side length a. The material unit weight is r. A concentrated downward axial load F is applied at point B on the column axis, where AB=3l/4 and BC=l/4. Take the positive x direction downward along the column axis. Considering self-weight, determine the axial-force distribution along the column and present the final axial-force diagram. Answer format: one final image only, with no extra text.

step_1.content: Let the cross-sectional area be A=a^2. With x measured downward from the top C, the self-weight per unit length is q_g=Ar=a^2r. The axial force N(x) is taken as positive in compression.
step_1.key_point: Use q_g=a^2r as the uniform distributed axial body-force intensity.

step_4.content:
On segment CB (0<=x<=l/4), only self-weight above the section is included:
N(x)=q_gx=a^2rx.
At B from the upper side:
N_{B^-}=a^2r\frac{l}{4}.
step_4.key_point: For CB, N(x) increases linearly from 0 with slope a^2r.

step_7.content:
At B, the concentrated force F causes a jump:
N_{B^+}=N_{B^-}+F=F+a^2r\frac{l}{4}.
On segment BA (l/4<=x<=l):
N(x)=F+q_gx=F+a^2rx.
At the base A (x=l):
N_A=F+a^2rl.
So the axial-force diagram is piecewise linear with slope a^2r everywhere and a positive jump of magnitude F at x=l/4.
step_7.key_point: Across B, N jumps by F; elsewhere N varies linearly with slope a^2r.
